title: Chapter 2: A Parking Lot Is Not a Sacred Grove
content: <!-- Speed: Breather → Sprint | D1+D5 -->
Motel 6, somewhere past Gilroy. 2:47 AM.

Chloe was sitting cross-legged on the hood of the Honda Civic, staring at the stars through a gap in the motel's neon sign. The sign was missing the letter "T" so it read "MO_EL 6," which Chloe had decided was ancient Elvish for "Sanctuary of the Weary."
Sam was inside, sitting on the edge of one of the twin beds, staring at her phone.
She'd been staring at it for forty minutes.
The screen showed her work email. Forty-seven unread messages. Her Slack had 112 notifications. Her calendar had a meeting at 9 AM tomorrow — *today* — titled "Q3 Budget Review (Mandatory)."
Her hands were shaking.

Not the dramatic kind of shaking. The quiet kind. The kind she'd learned to hide by holding her iced americano, which she was doing now, even though the ice had melted three hours ago and the cup was mostly condensation and regret.
She knew what was coming. She'd been tracking it for years.

The tightness in her chest. The way the motel room walls seemed to be moving inward, slowly, like a trash compactor in a Star Wars movie. The voice in her head that sounded exactly like her but meaner, cataloguing everything she'd done wrong today:

*You quit your job.*
*You didn't even give two weeks' notice.*
*You're in a Motel 6 near Gilroy.*
*You have a 401(k) you didn't roll over.*
*Your mother is going to call.*
*Your mother is going to CALL.*
The voice was especially loud about the mother part.

Sam's breathing was getting shallow. She recognized the pattern — Stage 2 of what she privately called The Spiral. Stage 1 was the tightness. Stage 3 was when she couldn't feel her fingers. She'd never let herself get to Stage 4 around anyone else.
She needed to get out of this room.

She pushed open the door. The parking lot was mostly empty — a pickup truck with a Confederate flag sticker (Chloe had classified it as "a barbarian war wagon"), a minivan with a "Baby on Board" sign ("a merchant family caravan"), and Shadowfax, gleaming dully under the MO_EL 6 sign like a horse that had been ridden too hard and needed oats.
Chloe was still on the hood.

She'd taken off her plastic crown — it was sitting beside her, catching the neon glow — and her hair was loose, tangled from the wind. She was tracing constellations with her finger, murmuring to herself.
"...and that one is the Serpent of Accounting, slain by the Hero of HR in the Third Quarter Rebellion..."
"Chloe."
Chloe turned. Saw Sam's face. Saw the tightness around her eyes, the way her fingers were curled into her palms, the slight tremor in her shoulders that meant Stage 2 was accelerating toward Stage 3.
Chloe slid off the hood immediately.

"Hey," she said, and her voice was completely different now — no queen, no protagonist, no fourth-wall winking. Just a 23-year-old woman who had stayed up all night with her best friend more times than she could count. "Hey. You're okay."
"I know," Sam said, which meant *I'm not okay at all.*
"Come here."

Sam came. Chloe pulled her onto the hood of the Civic. The metal was cold and slightly dented — Sam knew the exact dent, the one from the parking garage pillar in 2022, she'd been there — but right now it felt like the only stable surface in the universe.
"Breathe with me," Chloe said. "In for four. Hold for four. Out for four."
They'd done this before. Dozens of times. Hundreds. In Chloe's apartment, in Sam's cubicle, in the bathroom of a Cheesecake Factory, once in an IKEA display bedroom while a confused employee watched.
In. Hold. Out.
In. Hold. Out.
The motel sign buzzed. A truck passed on the highway, its rumble fading like a distant dragon.
"I quit my job," Sam said, when she could speak again.
"Yeah."
"I didn't give notice."
"Neither did I."
"I have a 401(k)."

"I have a sword."
Sam almost laughed. It didn't quite make it out — the laugh got stuck somewhere around her collarbone — but Chloe saw it. Chloe always saw it.
"You're insane," Sam said.
"Probably," Chloe agreed. "But I'm also right."
"About what?"

Chloe pointed at the sky. "Look. Those aren't stars. Those are the campfires of the Army of Avalon. Every single person who's ever felt like we feel right now — trapped, exhausted, told their ideas don't matter, told to be grateful for the privilege of being crushed — they're out there. Waiting for someone to light the signal."
"That's not how astronomy works."
"Astronomy is just fantasy that got a government grant."
Sam was quiet. Then: "What's the signal?"
Chloe's face split into a grin. She reached over and picked up her plastic crown. "This. And a 2008 Honda Civic."
The next morning, they hit the road at 7:14 AM. Sam had planned for 7:00 but the Honda Civic had needed a jump start.
"Where are we actually going?" Sam asked, merging onto the 101.
"South," Chloe said.
"South is a direction, not a destination."
"All great quests begin with a direction. The destination reveals itself."
Sam pulled up the spreadsheet on her phone while driving. Seven tabs: REST STOPS, MOTELS, GAS, EMERGENCY CONTACTS, POTENTIAL THREATS, BUDGET TRACKER, WORST CASE.

"The next rest stop with clean bathrooms is in forty-two miles," Sam announced. "The next motel under eighty dollars with a rating above three stars is in King City. We have enough gas for one hundred and eighty-seven miles. And if we get a flat tire, there's a AAA-approved garage in Paso Robles that's open until nine."
"Sam."
"What?"
"You're the most competent person I've ever met."
Sam's jaw tightened. "Don't."
"Don't what?"

"Don't —" Sam's fingers tightened on the steering wheel. "Don't say nice things right now. I'm still processing the whole 'quit my job with no notice' thing and if you're nice to me I might start crying and I can't cry while driving a 2008 Honda Civic on a highway."
Chloe reached over and turned up the music. Track seven: "Dog Days Are Over" by Florence + the Machine.
"Happiness hit her like a train on a track," Florence sang through the crackling speakers.
Sam's hands slowly relaxed on the wheel.
"Dog days are over," Sam muttered. "The dog days are definitely over."
"That's the spirit," Chloe said.
Sam took a breath. "I'm not saying I believe in Avalon."
"You don't have to. You just have to drive."
Another breath. "How far south?"
Chloe smiled. "Until we find it."
"Find what?"
"Whatever we're supposed to find."
Sam considered this. Then she opened the WORST CASE tab and added a new row: "No destination. No plan. Chloe is driving now (metaphorically)."
She colored the cell red.
And kept driving.

They drove through the golden hills in silence, the kind of silence that isn't empty but full — full of everything neither of them had said yet. The sun sank. The shadows stretched. The Honda Civic's check engine light glowed like a small, stubborn star.

"Sam?"
"Yeah?"
"Thanks for coming with me."
Sam didn't answer. She just turned the music up one more notch and pressed the accelerator a little harder.
